title: Quick question Re image display in Eva cockpit
display_title: Quick question Re image display in Eva cockpit
wikitext:
[]: Quick question Re: image display in Eva cockpit             Quote Post by V » Fri Dec 14, 2007 1:11 pm In an Eva cockpit we see an almost 360 degree display of an Eva, as if the pilot were situated in the Eva's head. Eva pilots don't see through the organic eyes of the Eva (well, not exclusively) but there are presumably little cameras throughout the armor that feed into the central nervous system. My question is: the Eva cockpits in the Entry Plug don't have video monitors in them, right? Thus....the monitors the kids see are being pumped into their mind...including information from simple cameras. Then why are those metal bars even there at all? Plus I mean, a video feed from Misato in control gets pumped right into their brain? Just double-checking.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Dec 14, 2007 1:25 pm Reichu and I had a good head-to-head over this one back on ANF ... it'll be in the archives, if not actually uploaded yet. I'm for the "it's fed directly to the pilot" since that matches up with the visual static in the early boot-up sequences.

[]: Re: Quick question Re: image display in Eva cockpit             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Dec 14, 2007 1:26 pm V wrote: My question is: the Eva cockpits in the Entry Plug don't have video monitors in them, right?

[]:             Quote Post by V » Fri Dec 14, 2007 1:52 pm Seeing as innate distrust of anyone over 40 is one of my defining characteristics I'll side with Reichu here. But I do not follow what you mean Reichu: the images (of Angels, trees, landscapes, etc.) are fed into the brain; the "message from Eva 00" or "Message from control center" screens (plus the "warning!" displays).....I think they're also fed right into the brain? there do not appear to be displays of any kind at all. Further, is clicking the triggers and hand-controls what actually does certain complex tasks, or are they "not hooked up to anything" and it is the neural action in the pilot's brain of "the part of the brain responsible for moving the trigger finger is activated, this is transmitted through the LCL mental link, and its actually the brain that's firing"?

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Fri Dec 14, 2007 3:43 pm Given the massive 3D displays inside headquarters, I don't think it's impossible that at least some of the Eva HUD stuff is holographic. Also, consider recent advances in digital paper and other forms of unconventional graphical display.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Fri Dec 14, 2007 5:08 pm BobBQ wrote: Given the massive 3D displays inside headquarters, I don't think it's impossible that at least some of the Eva HUD stuff is holographic. Also, consider recent advances in digital paper and other forms of unconventional graphical display.

[]:             Quote Post by Katayoku no Tenshi » Fri Dec 14, 2007 8:58 pm V wrote: the images (of Angels, trees, landscapes, etc.) are fed into the brain; the "message from Eva 00" or "Message from control center" screens (plus the "warning!" displays).....I think they're also fed right into the brain? there do not appear to be displays of any kind at all.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 15, 2007 6:08 pm I'm going with the "they're images pumped into their brain just like Eva info" theory.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sat Dec 15, 2007 6:35 pm Occam's Razor does not approve.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sat Dec 15, 2007 7:12 pm how so?

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sat Dec 15, 2007 8:52 pm Occam's Razor, our old friend! It's going to be way too convoluted to explain the idea of the images being pumped into their mind directly. There's a holographic telephone on the outside realistic enough for Shinji to flinch when Asuka bops him over the head, so why not create a holographic cockpit environment? The metal bars could serve a simple purpose- to prevent Vertigo in situations like free-fall, and serve as an aid in orientation. Besides, when focusing on Toji's crushed entry plug, Shinji uses a camera...and it's obviously monitor feed. And have you forgotten that the image display and text inside the Eva are identical to the monitors seen at Nerv?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Dec 16, 2007 3:12 am Did I mention that production drawings refer to the various displays inside the cockpit as "screens" and not "chunks of camera input converted into optical nervous information that's represented as a stylized and segmented wrap-around personal IMAX theater for no apparent reason"? Sorry, I can be a bit sardonic at times.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sun Dec 16, 2007 8:11 am Just to clarify the situation, we've got only 2 alternate scenarios, right?: 1-literally all images they see are fed directly into their brain (hey, they already feel everything the Eva feels) 2-while they do see things the Eva sees (supplemented by cameras across its body), video feeds from other Evas or command HQ can appear using holographic display projections. In either case, there aren't actual "screens" because we don't see screens when the Eva is off; plus we've seen them go away (they're like a popup window). The script just calls them screens. plus, in this case I think "Occam's Razor" would mean that as they're already pumping video input directly into the pilot's brain (they see what the Eva sees), logically they'd just pump video feeds into that rather than bothering to install a separate holographic display.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Sun Dec 16, 2007 8:25 am I'd like to take this moment to point out that the Evas eyes are generally covered. The yellowish things we normally see on EVA-01 are cameras. In light of this I doubt the Evas see much of anything most of the time. Something to keep in mind.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sun Dec 16, 2007 9:17 am Boo-yay! Anyway, those screens are directly connected to Eva sensory input of some sort, take a look at the berserking Eva-02 in EoE. The screens all start to flash and blur with lines of static, and then flare red with sparkly things when the true berserking mode activates. They also go black as the Eva as eaten. I suppose that the cameras and monitors are interfaced through the Eva's nervous system somehow, which explains why Sho didn't regenerate that eye off the bat when Sachiel poked it out. The cameras use the Eva's nervous systems instead of wiring to connect to the screens inside the entry plug (And that would explain some of the odd things, like static and flashing imagery, quite nicely)

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sun Dec 16, 2007 9:23 am Perhaps. However, piping digitally synthesized images into the brain is significantly more complex than simply transferring roughly homogeneous analog impulses from one nervous system to another, therefore Occam's Razor would suggest that the displays are a mixture of holographic projection and wraparound fixed screens.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Sun Dec 16, 2007 9:25 am Normal video input wouldn't explain those crazy images at the startup. Unfortunately, Evangelions regularly ignore basic common sense, especially when a whole lot of signals, including the entry plug eject signal, can be rejected by the Eva itself.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sun Dec 16, 2007 11:28 am "It's a giant living rock it doesn't have a vulnerable spot!!" --Galaxy Quest It's a big honkin' cloned alien cyborg! Make "sense"?! :) But it would seem better for combat efficiency and such for the pilot to be able to see all video "in real time" by pumping it straight into the nervous system.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Sun Dec 16, 2007 11:33 am V wrote: But it would seem better for combat efficiency and such for the pilot to be able to see all video "in real time" by pumping it straight into the nervous system.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Sun Dec 16, 2007 12:13 pm they were able to make the Eva series using mind machine interface, yes it is safe to assume they're sophisticated enough to do that.
